what_it_does: This file is a research-wave anchor and brief for a 'live, guided, right-hand-man review surface.' It frames the problem (a non-developer commander was confused by a detached mockup gallery), proposes an interaction model (a conversational, sequenced, RHM-led walkthrough with markup and a simple generate→approve→dispatch→git loop), lists existing machinery to reuse, flags hard parts, and instructs agents to ground, verify, and contradict it.
observations: The file explicitly rejects an earlier over-engineered consent model and insists on a single 'approve this batch' gate with git as the safety net.
It positions the user as a non-developer 'commander' who should not have to read code or specs.
The proposed surface is a live guided conversation, not a browsable gallery: NEXT/BACK stops, RHM narration, click-and-talk, deictic references, and accrued markup.
It distinguishes real-UI edits from new-UI mockups and says the surface must handle both.
It references many existing code symbols, API routes, and vault documents as candidate foundations.
It includes a numbered 'honest hard parts' section naming live grounding, altitude explanation, streaming/voice, RHM-driven view, plan composition, and mockup-vs-live routing.
It instructs readers to stress-test the anchor, contradict naive claims, and leave the idea 'bigger and more real.'
standouts: The upfront correction: no per-comment/per-element consent tiers — only one batch approval plus git revert.
The core reframing: review is a live guided conversation at the commander's altitude, not a mockup gallery.
The simple closed loop: generate → show → approve → dispatch → commit → revert if broken.
conventions: Uses a numbered-section research-wave anchor format (§0–§8).
Asks for evidence markers: Observed / Inferred / External-prior-art / Anchor-idea.
Cites code symbols with file:line references.
States guardrails explicitly, e.g., 'do not re-introduce elaborate gating.'
Mixes factual claims, inferred ideas, and open questions in one document.
concerns: Many claims are labeled as ideas needing verification, so the file is partly a design manifesto rather than a grounded specification.
It assumes a large body of prior-session context and vault documents that are not reproduced here.
It bundles ambitious capabilities (live streaming, voice-in, deictic/temporal grounding, RHM-driven view) without showing how they are currently achievable.
The 'expansion ratio > 1' instruction is evocative but operationally undefined.
notes: A research-wave anchor that tries to turn a failed mockup gallery into a live, RHM-guided review conversation for a non-developer commander, while forbidding elaborate consent gating.
questions: What exactly is the current state of the 'detached studio scaffold' it says failed?
Which prior-session vault documents map to each design claim in §4 and §5?
How does the proposed live, streamed, talk-back dialogue differ from the existing request/response chat organ?
What do 'ui://' addresses and R2 context resolution concretely enable for deixis?
Is voice input in scope or merely a 'what-if'?
What does 'expansion ratio > 1' mean as a deliverable criterion?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — it is explicitly a research-wave anchor and agent brief, though it also functions as a design manifesto full of unverified ideas.
apparent_themes: Commander altitude: the interface must explain itself to someone who has not read the code.
Simplicity over over-engineering, especially in consent.
Grounding in existing machinery before building new.
Honest rigor about the hardest parts.
Git as the safety net for a single approval batch.
intention_signals: It is trying to rally a research wave around building a non-developer-friendly, RHM-led review surface that turns conversation and on-screen markup into autonomous build batches, while explicitly avoiding the prior mistake of elaborate consent gating.
novelty: It frames design review as a guided, conversational, deictic, time-aware walkthrough rather than a gallery, and it explicitly invites agents to contradict the brief itself.
contribution: It contributes a clear problem framing, a high-level interaction model (sequence → narrate → dialogue → markup → generate → approve → dispatch), a map of candidate existing components to investigate, and a set of hard questions for the research wave to answer.
summary_for_embedding: A research-wave anchor for a live, conversational, RHM-guided review surface intended to make a non-developer commander's design review intelligible without reading code.
intention_for_embedding: To guide agents in grounding, verifying, and possibly correcting a proposed guided-review surface design while reusing existing Company machinery and keeping consent simple.
